There's a difference of opinion about the past. So Chloe, in that situation, and Mark, in that situation, do you think it's necessary for the adult sibling to apologize for the pain they caused and not either supporting, believing, or trying to repair the relationship, the larger family relationship? I guess that there are situations when that would be appropriate, but what I have come across so often is false memories where one sibling says this kind of thing happened and the other sibling says, absolutely no, it could not have happened. This did not happen. And those are very difficult to bridge. You need to bring the opinion of other people who were there at the time who were alive. And sometimes what I've gotten into is some middle of the road thing, like something happened. It wasn't as bad as one sibling says it was, but it wasn't nothing like the other sibling says it was nothing. Right, right. A great example, a great example of perspective like that that I always am reminded of when we talk about this is a girlfriend of mine who told me she came from a family of five children. And as an adult speaking to her brother one day, they realized that for her brother, the day that their house was cleaned, someone came to their house once a week to clean it. For him, that was the best day of the week. He returned from being out after school and found everything looked great. His mother was in a great mood. It was wonderful. And for my girlfriend, she remembered that it was the worst day of the week. She had to stay out of her house while it was cleaned. She felt that she was being forced out of her home. When she came back, everything was put in a different place. It made her feel that her parents didn't care enough to do the cleaning themselves. Neither did her brothers. It was a completely different picture. So I love that example because it doesn't involve abuse at all. It's just a simple act of cleaning a house. Yeah, but it's totally different interpretation. Totally different interpretation. So it's important for us to remember as humans we all have such strong filters, but one of the things we strive for in helping our coaching clients is to want to experience the other's perspective. So that before one can actually want to apologize or do repair or be the person in the family that brings the family together, they have to first have the skill of wanting to understand the other person, wanting to understand the other perspectives. So sometimes the coaching sessions will revolve around finding out a client's key relationships and helping that client understand things from the other's perspective. That's a great point because you can't have a forced apology. Like, you know, in the schoolyard where they say sorry, you know, I mean, you know, sorry is that kind of thing. It wouldn't work. And so that's the real art of this type of intervention. And of course it's simpler with the parent-child relationship because there's usually a very deep connection and desire to have that relationship where there's a very deep love there on some level. And that's different with different types of family relationships and with siblings. So moving forward a little bit here, I just wanted to touch upon a few other points that came up during the next sessions we saw with the family members. And this can be looked at as sort of an over-overlying outline on how you can do a family session. So in this case, the couple first presents their journey of growth, how their relationship is wonderful now, the recovery they've had. Then they express their financial situation with honesty. Iran goes into very full details about their finances. They talk about what they've lost. They also talk about the goals they have, what they owe to the family, and what they need to do to move forward. And after doing all of that, then the focus becomes, in this case, becomes about the desires versus the reality. And this is something we really need to watch out for, I think, as coaches, is that it's easy to enter into a conversation with a client all about their desires and ignore their reality. But we're really not serving them when we do this. So Tony puts this into perspective very well by talking about how Marie might have to do something she doesn't really like, but she has the resources for, like, nursing, in order to be ready to jump into the career she wants when it presents itself. So some of the questions you can ask as a coach is, what has to happen to do that? Tony also explained a lot about how much Marie and Ron learned and how anyone can learn about their own situation by comparing themselves to others. And he used the metaphor of the seasons to talk about different times in life. So when life is hard, it's winter. In the fall, you reap the benefits of the summer. The spring is looking forward to things. So by talking about a very difficult stage of life as a season, it naturally gives the person hope that, of course, the season will change. Seasons disappear and they go into the next season. I think this really helps the client move forward to be able to think about how they will do what they have to do now and then in the future they'll have another season. Asking the family for advice is very important. So the coach prepares the family for their coaching of the couple by asking, what do you want to tell them? What do you want to ask them? This is when we need you to participate. He's very clearly, the coach can very clearly ask for the family's coaching of the couple in that situation. And in this case, we get to experience the father's perspective. And I didn't understand watching it that that was the stepfather. Was that the stepfather, Chloe? I think so, yes. So in his words, both needed to work outside of the home. But he also thought it was important that both of them step up to share the task at home. A job will happen, may not be exactly what they expect. And the coach can take a definition that one person has presented and asked for more feedback from other families, other family members about what that one person suggested. So this happened in this case. Tony asked for more perspectives and suggestions of how to make this happen. And I really felt that this father or stepfather understood Marie in a really beautiful way. He did. He really did. He was a totally non-judgmental person and totally uncritical. And he really understood her whereas the mother didn't. Right, right. I think she was maybe a little bit more like him. And he had had the experience of working with her and could really speak to how competent she could be and what a great employee and how great she was in the business world. Which obviously wasn't the perception of herself which she had held while being married and wasn't really how Ron had supported her or her female relatives had supported her. So it was great to hear. And that's one of the things that happens if you bring a whole family together. Someone who really gets that person will step up. Where in normal life they may not. He might have always just gone with the mother's advice, supporting his wife. He was careful that the stepfather did not intrude too much. Right, but in that setting it was very appropriate for him to get his feet back. So it's important for the coach to point out how sometimes we need to do what we don't like so that we're ready to grab what we do like. And this is really that principle of giving your client a reality check instead of buying into their desires and their dreams always. So the grandmother speaks, or I'm not sure if it was a great aunt or a grandmother. Great aunt, I think it was a great aunt. I know, you know what, I think it was the mother because then the grandmother spoke about being a child in that house when her mother needed to sleep a lot. But I'm not sure. No, they were all close. Family gives the confirmation of their beliefs in both Ron and Marie. And that that belief gives the individuals a lot of extra push and strength to move forward into a hard stage of life. I just wanted to end. Sorry, go ahead, Mark. Your point about the stepfather I think is really powerful because very often if you're a coach, you'll have people who have gotten into a conflict or dispute, right, and so a disagreement. So Marie was in two of those. She was in a disagreement with the husband and the husband was, you know, she felt like he should take care of her. She felt like, well, if you went and got work, it would be a piddly kind of work. So he was kind of pretty insulting about her capacity, even though my guess is that when they were doing well, she was really helping him direct and she probably had a lot of practical instincts on what would work in their business or in their work. And then secondly, you have she was in a conflict with her mother who thought that she should just buck up and become a nurse. So often you'll have people who get stuck in these oppositions. And as a coach, you really looking for that third perspective from someone who knows them. And it's most powerful when it's someone who has insight into the strengths or special qualities of people. So I think in my opinion, that was actually the biggest takeaway from that was the father-in-law who can look at her and say, you know what, I have a totally different experience of Marie. And then suddenly that opposition gets dissolved in this current perspective. And you should always look for that when you have two people who they don't see eye to eye, they have a fight, or they have an ongoing disagreement. Find someone else's perspective into their history, into the nature, into the talents of the people, into the context, and get that third perspective that will allow you to break that up. Then she can really make a smart choice. And it turns out that she was good in the accounting stuff and the business stuff that she had a good head on her shoulders and was in the right direction for her. Right. And you know, one thing I wanted to add here to the discussion is that I think I have a lot of personal experience with, and that I see a lot in women I know in their 40s, is that we were the first generation that had our mothers work. And for all the, you know, importance, and I'm a feminist, and I think it's fantastic, obviously, that my mother had a great career. But many of us wanted them to be different from our mothers. So Marie speaks to this early on in one of the early interventions that one of her reasons for staying home was to be a different kind of mother. And so she was differentiating from her mother in her own mothering very strongly, so probably going into the same profession was difficult for her. And as coaches, we will, I find, and I'm sure many of you find, that this is a dilemma for many people about how they will parent, will they parent the way their parents did, or will they make another choice? And that's been a bit of a shift in culture that women feel that to stay home is a very empowering thing to do, versus feeling empowered by being able to work, which was our mothers and our grandmothers' situation. So, just to think about when you're seeing women in their 30s or 20s or 40s who are making that decision to stay home, and sometimes it doesn't seem to make sense financially or emotionally to the family. But there might be a background there. Well, one thing that is universal is that women always struggle between career, family, children, and it's difficult to find the perfect balance.
